label: delphi-r2-p4
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would hold that the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise were public records under the CPRA, and that the city's "no responsive records" response, made without any inquiry to the members about personal-account communications, violated the Act."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 76,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "Starting from the strongest lawful-conduct position \u2014 that the clerk reasonably searched \"city systems\" and had no actual knowledge of a private Signal channel \u2014 that defense mostly collapses on these facts. It is very likely a reviewing court holds the Signal content was a \"public record\" under San Jose (content, not device, controls), and very likely finds the \"no responsive records\" answer violated the CPRA, both because the request itself demanded \"any platform or account\" (giving actual notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient) and because at least one councilmember concededly still held ~40 responsive screenshots when the answer was given, making \"no responsive records\" false as to existing material. The compound proposition is strong but not certain because CPRA search-duty doctrine for personal-device records remains somewhat unsettled post-San Jose, and a court could still characterize the clerk's initial ignorance of the group's existence as a reasonable search \"at the time,\" blunting (though not eliminating) a violation finding.",
  "rationales": [
    "San Jose holds that the content of a writing, not the device, account, or personal ownership, determines whether it is a \"public record,\" and franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing are core \"conduct of the public's business,\" so the Signal discussions very likely qualify.",
    "The CPRA request expressly sought communications \"on any platform or account,\" which gave the clerk actual notice that a search confined to city servers could not satisfy the Act's good-faith reasonable-search standard.",
    "The \"no responsive records\" answer was demonstrably inaccurate the moment it was given, since one councilmember still possessed roughly 40 screenshots of responsive material, which alone likely establishes a violation independent of the broader public-record/search-methodology questions.",
    "San Jose remanded specifically for development of a reasonable search methodology, and emerging post-remand practice generally requires agencies to inquire of individual custodians once on notice that personal-device records may exist, so a search with zero inquiry to any councilmember falls short of that developing standard.",
    "Countervailing uncertainty remains because a court could find some or all content is preliminary/deliberative and thus exempt from disclosure even though it is a \"public record,\" and could excuse the clerk's initial search as reasonable given no proof the clerk had actual knowledge of the Signal group before this dispute, which would soften a violation finding on the search-adequacy prong even if it does not eliminate it."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "Gov. Code \u00a7\u00a7 7920.000 et seq. (CPRA, recodified from former \u00a7 6250 et seq.) \u2014 verify current section numbers",
    "City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 7922.545 / former \u00a7 6254(a) (preliminary drafts/notes exemption) \u2014 verify recodified citation",
    "ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search adequacy)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 34090 (records destruction/retention authority for local agencies)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 7923.100 / former \u00a7 6258 (CPRA judicial enforcement \u2014 mandate/injunction/declaratory relief)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 7923.115 / former \u00a7 6259 (fee-shifting for prevailing CPRA petitioners) \u2014 relevant to remedies question"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether California courts will treat \"zero inquiry to individual officials\" as per se unreasonable search under the CPRA absent a settled bright-line duty to canvass personal devices \u2014 the precise scope of the post-San Jose search-methodology obligation is still doctrinally underdeveloped, so a court could instead frame this as a forward-looking remedial issue rather than a clear violation at the time the response was given."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 78.0, mean 76.9, std dev 3.2, range 70.0–80.0, middle 50% of panel between 76.0 and 80.0.

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — Content-controls-not-location (San Jose prong one).** *7 of 7 panelists.* Every panelist grounded the "public record" determination in *City of San Jose*'s content-based test, and every panelist characterized the subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will accept," RFP timing) as core conduct of the public's business.

**Theme B — Duty to inquire of individual custodians / reasonable-search standard.** *7 of 7 panelists.* All invoked some version of a reasonably-calculated or reasonable-diligence search standard and noted the clerk's failure to ask any of the three councilmembers about personal-account communications. Panelists differed on the doctrinal source (San Jose remand instructions, § 6253.1/§ 7922.600 duty-to-assist, AB 700, case law on search adequacy) — see Theme E and Disagreement point 2.

**Theme C — "Any platform or account" request language as notice.** *5 of 7 panelists* (probabilities 70, 76, 78, 78, 80). These panelists argued the request's express wording put the clerk on notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, with some describing the resulting search as "facially inadequate" or a candidate for a per-se violation.

**Theme D — The ~40 surviving screenshots.** *7 of 7 panelists, but used in two distinct ways:*
- *6 panelists* (76 through 80) treated the screenshots as making "no responsive records" factually inaccurate at the moment it was given — an independent basis for a violation regardless of the deleted messages.
- *1 panelist* (70) used the screenshots differently: as evidentiary proof of content and participation that allows a court to reach a definitive holding on the public-record question, rather than as proof of response falsity.

**Theme E — AB 700 statutory codification of personal-account search duties.** *2 of 7 panelists* (76, 78) cited AB 700 as codifying an affirmative duty to route requests to individual officials — though with different section numbers and chapter citations (see Disagreement point 5).

**Theme F — Conjunctive fragility of the proposition.** *5 of 7 panelists* (70, 76, 78, 78, 80) expressly noted that the proposition requires both prongs to hold, making it more fragile than either prong alone; the second (search-adequacy) prong was uniformly identified as the weaker link among these panelists.

**Theme G — Clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge as a countervailing defense.** *3 of 7 panelists* (76, 76, 78) — a mid-range cluster — flagged that a court could excuse the initial search as reasonable if the clerk had no actual or constructive notice of the Signal group at the time of response.

**Theme H — Majority-bloc participation strengthens the public-business nexus.** *3 of 7 panelists* (78, 80, 80) — skewing toward higher-probability panelists — emphasized that three of five councilmembers (a voting majority) participating in substantive deliberation reinforces both the content nexus and the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element.

**Theme I — Deleted-messages scope problem.** *2 of 7 panelists* (both at 80 — the highest-probability panelists) raised the possibility that a court might limit its "public records" holding to the surviving screenshots and treat the bulk of auto-deleted messages as a retention/spoliation issue rather than a CPRA determination.

**Theme J — Availability of declaratory relief despite destruction.** *1 panelist* (76) noted that CPRA declaratory/injunctive relief does not require that responsive records presently exist.

**Theme K — Possible exemptions even if records qualify.** *1 panelist* (76) noted content could be found preliminary/deliberative and exempt from disclosure despite public-record status.

**Theme L — Third-party/consultant-hosted platform distinction.** *1 panelist* (70 — the lowest-probability panelist) flagged that San Jose involved the official's own texts/emails, whereas this involves a private bidder's consultant-run disappearing-message platform, with no published California decision on point.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **What triggers the duty to inquire of individual officials.** Some panelists treat the request's "any platform or account" language as itself sufficient to trigger the duty (making the city-systems-only search per se or facially inadequate); others posit that absent actual or constructive knowledge of the Signal group, the search could be found reasonable "at the time." Several panelists hold both positions in tension within their own answers, but as between panelists the trigger assumption is incompatible: request-language-alone versus knowledge-required.

2. **The state of post-San Jose search-methodology doctrine.** The 70 panelist cites the 2018 Court of Appeal remand decision (*City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013) as published authority contemplating that agencies must ask custodians to search personal accounts. Other panelists (78, 78, 80) describe search methodology as expressly left unresolved by San Jose, with no binding appellate authority fixing the duty's contours. These are incompatible readings of the same doctrinal landscape.

3. **Scope of any "public records" holding as to the deleted messages.** The two 80 panelists assume a court might confine its holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and recharacterize the rest as a retention/spoliation matter; other panelists' analyses assume the full body of Signal messages is subject to the public-record determination.

4. **Whether the Signal platform's characteristics matter.** The 70 panelist treats the consultant-initiated, third-party-hosted, ephemeral nature of the channel as a genuine open question under the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element; other panelists (78, 80) describe these facts as a *cleaner* fit for San Jose than San Jose's own facts, treating the platform's characteristics as immaterial.

5. **What Gov. Code § 7922.545 is.** One panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the AB 700 personal-account search provision (Stats. 2021, ch. 132); another panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the recodified preliminary-drafts exemption (former § 6254(a)); a third panelist (78) cites the AB 700 provision as § 6252.5 (Stats. 2021, ch. 456). These citations are mutually inconsistent as to section number and chapter, and multiple panelists flagged their own citations for verification.

6. **Per se violation versus fact-bound adequacy question.** Panelists split on whether the "no responsive records" response would be treated as a clean statutory violation or as a fact-intensive search-adequacy question on which the city could partially prevail through a good-faith showing.

## 4. Authorities Cited

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 34090 (local agency records retention/destruction) — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6252(e) / § 7920.530 (definition of "public records") — **4 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253.1 / § 7922.600 (duty to assist requester) — **3 panelists**
- *ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court* (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search adequacy) — **3 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253 / § 7922.500 / § 7922.530 (duty to make records available) — **3 panelists**
- AB 700 personal-account search provisions (cited variously as § 7922.545 and § 6252.5) — **2 panelists** (with inconsistent section/chapter citations; see Disagreement point 5)
- CPRA judicial-enforcement provisions (§ 7923.100, cited variously as former § 6259 and former § 6258) — **2 panelists**

**Cited by one panelist each:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand decision)
- *Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385
- *Anderson-Barker v. Superior Court* (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 528 (flagged by the citing panelist as an analog to verify)
- Gov. Code § 7920.545 / former § 6252(g) (definition of "writing")
- Gov. Code § 7922.545 / former § 6254(a) (cited as preliminary drafts/notes exemption)
- Gov. Code § 7923.115 / former § 6259 (fee-shifting)
- Gov. Code § 7920.000 et seq. (general CPRA recodification citation)

Several panelists flagged their own recodified section numbers for verification; those flags are preserved above without correction.

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":79,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Reconsidering independently, the compound proposition holds up slightly better than my round-1 estimate. Prong one (public-record status) is very strong: San Jose's content-controls-not-location rule squarely covers franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing, and the fact that three of five councilmembers — a voting majority — were jointly discussing \"what council will realistically accept\" makes this look more like collective conduct of public business than San Jose's own facts (individual staffer texts), not less. Prong two (violation) is also strong, not primarily because of an unsettled search-methodology doctrine, but because the \"no responsive records\" answer was objectively false the moment it was given: a councilmember held ~40 responsive screenshots at that time, regardless of whether the clerk's city-systems-only search was independently unreasonable. That factual-falsity route to a violation finding is less doctrinally fragile than the search-adequacy route and is largely immune to the \"clerk lacked actual knowledge\" defense, since the existence of responsive material — not the clerk's diligence — is what makes the response inaccurate. I remain short of the 80s because a residual, non-trivial chance exists that a court (a) treats deliberative/preliminary-draft exemptions as covering some content even though it's a public record, (b) confines any public-record holding to the surviving screenshots and routes the deleted bulk into a retention/spoliation lane rather than a clean CPRA violation, or (c) is persuaded that the consultant-hosted, third-party ephemeral platform presents a genuinely open question San Jose didn't resolve.","rationales":["San Jose's content-based test (2 Cal.5th 608) is doctrinally settled and squarely reached by these facts; the panel's unanimous reliance on this theme confirms it as the least contestable part of the analysis, so prong one alone likely clears 85-90%.","The majority-bloc fact (3 of 5 councilmembers, a Brown Act-quorum-sized group, discussing 'what council will accept') strengthens the public-business characterization beyond San Jose's own facts — this is not personal chatter among staff but coordinated engagement by a governing-body majority on matters within their official authority, which I underweighted in round 1.","The ~40 surviving screenshots provide an independent, fact-based route to a violation finding that sidesteps the harder search-methodology doctrine entirely: 'no responsive records' was false as stated at the time it was given, and that falsity does not depend on resolving whether zero-inquiry-to-custodians is per se unreasonable under an unsettled post-San Jose standard.","Countervailing risk persists on scope: a court could confine any 'public records' holding to the provable surviving screenshots and treat the deleted majority of messages as a retention/spoliation question rather than folding them wholesale into the CPRA violation finding, which would make the proposition's sweeping phrasing ('the councilmembers' Signal messages... were public records') only partially correct as to the deleted portion.","The AB 700 personal-account-search-duty theme surfaced by two panelists is undermined by their own inconsistent section citations (§ 7922.545 vs. § 6252.5, different chapter numbers), so I decline to lean on it as a doctrinal anchor without independent verification of the correct codification."],"authorities":["City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608","City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand on search methodology) — verify holding and precedential weight on personal-device inquiry duty","ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search adequacy standard)","Gov. Code § 7920.530 / former § 6252(e) (definition of 'public records')","Gov. Code § 7922.545 (preliminary drafts/notes exemption, former § 6254(a)) — verify this is not conflated with any AB 700 provision","Gov. Code § 34090 (local agency records retention/destruction authority)","Gov. Code § 7923.115 / former § 6259 (fee-shifting for prevailing CPRA petitioners)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a reviewing court treats the objective falsity of the \"no responsive records\" answer (given the existing 40 screenshots) as sufficient by itself to find a CPRA violation, or instead requires a separate finding that the clerk's search methodology was unreasonable at the time — and relatedly, whether any public-records holding would extend to the auto-deleted bulk of messages or be confined to the surviving screenshots, leaving the deleted portion to a retention/spoliation analysis outside the core CPRA violation finding.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"Two themes genuinely moved me upward from 76 to 79: (1) Theme D's near-unanimous framing (6/7) of the surviving screenshots as making the response factually false independent of search-methodology debates — this is a more robust violation theory than the one I emphasized in round 1, since it isn't vulnerable to the 'clerk lacked actual knowledge' defense I had flagged as my chief source of doubt; and (2) Theme H's point about majority-bloc (3-of-5) participation, which I had not weighted — it strengthens the public-business characterization beyond San Jose's own facts. I did not defer to the panel median mechanically; I declined to move higher despite the median/mean sitting at 78/76.9 because Theme I (scope-limiting risk, raised only by the two highest-probability panelists) persuades me there's a genuine residual chance a court partitions the holding between surviving and deleted messages, which cuts against treating the full proposition as clean. I also declined to adopt AB 700 as a supporting authority given the panel's own internal citation conflict (Disagreement point 5), treating that theme as too unverified to lean on."}

data.position_summary: Reconsidering independently, the compound proposition holds up slightly better than my round-1 estimate. Prong one (public-record status) is very strong: San Jose's content-controls-not-location rule squarely covers franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing, and the fact that three of five councilmembers — a voting majority — were jointly discussing "what council will realistically accept" makes this look more like collective conduct of public business than San Jose's own facts (individual staffer texts), not less. Prong two (violation) is also strong, not primarily because of an unsettled search-methodology doctrine, but because the "no responsive records" answer was objectively false the moment it was given: a councilmember held ~40 responsive screenshots at that time, regardless of whether the clerk's city-systems-only search was independently unreasonable. That factual-falsity route to a violation finding is less doctrinally fragile than the search-adequacy route and is largely immune to the "clerk lacked actual knowledge" defense, since the existence of responsive material — not the clerk's diligence — is what makes the response inaccurate. I remain short of the 80s because a residual, non-trivial chance exists that a court (a) treats deliberative/preliminary-draft exemptions as covering some content even though it's a public record, (b) confines any public-record holding to the surviving screenshots and routes the deleted bulk into a retention/spoliation lane rather than a clean CPRA violation, or (c) is persuaded that the consultant-hosted, third-party ephemeral platform presents a genuinely open question San Jose didn't resolve.
rationales: San Jose's content-based test (2 Cal.5th 608) is doctrinally settled and squarely reached by these facts; the panel's unanimous reliance on this theme confirms it as the least contestable part of the analysis, so prong one alone likely clears 85-90%.

The majority-bloc fact (3 of 5 councilmembers, a Brown Act-quorum-sized group, discussing 'what council will accept') strengthens the public-business characterization beyond San Jose's own facts — this is not personal chatter among staff but coordinated engagement by a governing-body majority on matters within their official authority, which I underweighted in round 1.

The ~40 surviving screenshots provide an independent, fact-based route to a violation finding that sidesteps the harder search-methodology doctrine entirely: 'no responsive records' was false as stated at the time it was given, and that falsity does not depend on resolving whether zero-inquiry-to-custodians is per se unreasonable under an unsettled post-San Jose standard.

Countervailing risk persists on scope: a court could confine any 'public records' holding to the provable surviving screenshots and treat the deleted majority of messages as a retention/spoliation question rather than folding them wholesale into the CPRA violation finding, which would make the proposition's sweeping phrasing ('the councilmembers' Signal messages... were public records') only partially correct as to the deleted portion.

The AB 700 personal-account-search-duty theme surfaced by two panelists is undermined by their own inconsistent section citations (§ 7922.545 vs. § 6252.5, different chapter numbers), so I decline to lean on it as a doctrinal anchor without independent verification of the correct codification.
authorities: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608
City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand on search methodology) — verify holding and precedential weight on personal-device inquiry duty
ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search adequacy standard)
Gov. Code § 7922.545 (preliminary drafts/notes exemption, former § 6254(a)) — verify this is not conflated with any AB 700 provision
Gov. Code § 34090 (local agency records retention/destruction authority)
Gov. Code § 7923.115 / former § 6259 (fee-shifting for prevailing CPRA petitioners)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a reviewing court treats the objective falsity of the "no responsive records" answer (given the existing 40 screenshots) as sufficient by itself to find a CPRA violation, or instead requires a separate finding that the clerk's search methodology was unreasonable at the time — and relatedly, whether any public-records holding would extend to the auto-deleted bulk of messages or be confined to the surviving screenshots, leaving the deleted portion to a retention/spoliation analysis outside the core CPRA violation finding.
data.response_to_panel: Two themes genuinely moved me upward from 76 to 79: (1) Theme D's near-unanimous framing (6/7) of the surviving screenshots as making the response factually false independent of search-methodology debates — this is a more robust violation theory than the one I emphasized in round 1, since it isn't vulnerable to the 'clerk lacked actual knowledge' defense I had flagged as my chief source of doubt; and (2) Theme H's point about majority-bloc (3-of-5) participation, which I had not weighted — it strengthens the public-business characterization beyond San Jose's own facts. I did not defer to the panel median mechanically; I declined to move higher despite the median/mean sitting at 78/76.9 because Theme I (scope-limiting risk, raised only by the two highest-probability panelists) persuades me there's a genuine residual chance a court partitions the holding between surviving and deleted messages, which cuts against treating the full proposition as clean. I also declined to adopt AB 700 as a supporting authority given the panel's own internal citation conflict (Disagreement point 5), treating that theme as too unverified to lean on.
